topic: The British Experience of Warfare c1790-1918
subject: history

items.q: What were the Wars of the Roses really about?
items.correct: Dynastic conflict between rival branches of the Plantagenets (Lancaster and York) over the throne, intensified by weak kingship under Henry VI and noble factionalism.
items.misconception: The whole country was at war for thirty continuous years.
items.random_wrong: They were a religious war between Catholics and Protestants.
items.why: Fighting was sporadic and most of England saw no battles; ordinary life carried on. Students imagine total civil war; in reality, it was an aristocratic dispute fought in short campaigns.

items.q: Why did Hitler order the Night of the Long Knives in 1934?
items.correct: To eliminate Ernst Röhm and the SA leadership, reassure the army of his authority, and remove rivals — securing the army's loyalty when Hindenburg died weeks later.
items.misconception: Hitler purged the SA because they were Communists.
items.random_wrong: It was a battle in which the SA tried to overthrow Hitler.
items.why: The SA were Nazis, not Communists. Students miss that this was internal consolidation: Hitler traded the SA for army backing, completing the move to total power.

items.q: Did the Battle of Bosworth in 1485 end the Wars of the Roses?
items.correct: It killed Richard III and put Henry Tudor on the throne, but Yorkist threats (Simnel 1487, Warbeck 1490s) continued — the wars only truly ended with Stoke Field in 1487.
items.misconception: Bosworth in 1485 ended the Wars of the Roses immediately.
items.random_wrong: Bosworth was a naval battle fought off the south coast of England.
items.why: Henry VII faced rebellions for over a decade. Students learn 1485 as a tidy endpoint; in fact securing the dynasty took further fighting and ruthless control of pretenders.

items.q: Who was murdered with Liebknecht in January 1919?
items.why: Correct answer: Luxemburg. “Eisner” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did the Wannsee Conference coordinate?
items.why: It's Final Solution. “Operation Barbarossa” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the Dawes Plan agreed?
items.why: The real figure is 1924 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
